body_html:
[Image description: A graphic header. To the left is a logo consisting of a white mountain silhouette over a gray jagged mountain silhouette, flanked by two stylized trees, with a crosshair/compass symbol overlaying the lower left side. A large black dot sits to the left of the logo. To the right of the logo is the large, bold title text "Bearing 315". Below the title, a horizontal line separates the header from the sub-header.]

The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
July - September 2001

Editor’s Note

It is time for another issue already. I am always looking for stories or articles that would interest you, the reader. This issue as it is coming together incorporates adventure racing and orienteering. I have a feeling most of you have heard of adventure racing or at least the Eco-Challenge. Many people probably remember this years race in Borneo. But many of you may not know that a number of people in our club are very active in the sport of adventure racing. Orienteering is one of the most basic skills that these athletes need to compete, without a good navigator you will never finish. Just like life without help navigating we all will get a little lost from time to time. You can email your contributions to me at yeagerjustin@hotmail.com or contact me by phone (253-709-8232).

Gorge Games

Adventure racers from the pacific northwest and COC converged on Hood River to compete in the first Gorge Games Adventure Race. Where teams of 3 competed in a grueling multi-sport race consisting of mountaineering, trekking, mountain biking, kayaking, rappelling, and navigation. An early morning start catapulted the racers toward mount hood and the first checkpoint, high up on the mountain. After 30+ miles of mountaineering and trekking the teams would saddle their bikes and continues another 40 miles to the start of the kayak leg. Where strong winds and huge swells would once again challenge the racers stamina. Just under 13 hours after the start the first team crossed the finish line and 20 hours later the race ended with the last team crossing the finish.
Adventure racing takes good navigators and COC and other orienteering clubs have helped train adventure racers world wide. If you would like more information on adventure racing feel free to contact Justin Yeager at yeagerjustin@hotmail.com

\ Executive Board Member*

WUHOO! Adventure Race

Approximately 200 racers from as far away as Hong Kong will be competing against each other and mother nature near Roslyn, Washington this summer. Washington’s very first adventure race entitled The Wuhoo! is scheduled for August 25, 2001.

Adventure racing is a multi-sport event that requires a combination of active outdoor skills such as canoeing, mountain biking, trekking, and orienteering. Labeled as the fastest growing sport in the world. The idea of combining wilderness terrain with an endurance race is capturing the interest of athletes all over North America, as it is an easy cross over for orienteers, mountain bikers, and paddlers.

The Wuhoo! Adventure Race is one in a series of six Wuhoo! races. More information about the series can be found at www.wuhooadventurerace.com

Catch the next Wuhoo adventure race this winter.

Watch for the results of COC members who competed in the Wuhoo in the next issue of Bearing 315!

Meet Results | Cascade’s 2001 Ultimate Orienteer Series

Ultimate Orienteer 4

Teanaway West Long-O

Cle Elum

April 28, 2001

Event Director: Don Atkinson
Courses: Eric Bone
Registration: Dave Petty
Marker Pickup: Eric Bone, Dale Width, Don Atkinson

Eric Bone set some challenging courses on the previously unused western section of the Teanaway Forks area. Courses 3 and 4 had a mass start at 11 am. Course 3 was the same as course 4 for the first 3 controls.

Salmon Days

Salmon Days Are Here Again. If it is October and it is Issaquah, it is Salmon Days, and that means orienteering at Sammamish State Park. The date: Sunday, September 30, starts from 10 to 12. This park was also remapped last summer by Taavi Tatsi. He is a world ranked orienteer from Estonia, and will be here in September. The Park is just north of I 90 at Issaquah. Take the first Issaquah exit north, and you are at the Park. Entrance is about 50 meters to the west. Contact: Dave Enger (425) 822-6254

Volunteer Coordinator

If you like to talk on the phone, we have the perfect job for you. We always need people to help at meets and an organized, efficient way to get them. The Volunteer Coordinator is a critical position in the club, and one that we have done without too long. If you would like more information about this key position, please contact Debbie Newell (425-488-3691, dnewel@gte.net).

Meet Volunteers Sought

As you have probably noticed, each of our meets requires several volunteers in order to ensure it runs like a well-oiled machine, and we are always looking for people to help out. If you don’t feel confident enough directing a meet or setting courses, we can use your help at the registration, start, or finish areas. All of these jobs require minimal training and only take an hour or two at the meet. To volunteer, contact Debbie Newell (425-488-3691, dnewel@gte.net).

block_index.text: [Image description: A graphic header. To the left is a logo consisting of a white mountain silhouette over a gray jagged mountain silhouette, flanked by two stylized trees, with a crosshair/compass symbol overlaying the lower left side. A large black dot sits to the left of the logo. To the right of the logo is the large, bold title text "Bearing 315". Below the title, a horizontal line separates the header from the sub-header.]
The newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club July - September 2001

It is time for another issue already. I am always looking for stories or articles that would interest you, the reader. This issue as it is coming together incorporates adventure racing and orienteering. I have a feeling most of you have heard of adventure racing or at least the Eco-Challenge. Many people probably remember this years race in Borneo. But many of you may not know that a number of people in our club are very active in the sport of adventure racing. Orienteering is one of the most basic skills that these athletes need to compete, without a good navigator you will never finish. Just like life without help navigating we all will get a little lost from time to time. You can email your contributions to me at yeagerjustin@hotmail.com or contact me by phone (253-709-8232).

Adventure racers from the pacific northwest and COC converged on Hood River to compete in the first Gorge Games Adventure Race. Where teams of 3 competed in a grueling multi-sport race consisting of mountaineering, trekking, mountain biking, kayaking, rappelling, and navigation. An early morning start catapulted the racers toward mount hood and the first checkpoint, high up on the mountain. After 30+ miles of mountaineering and trekking the teams would saddle their bikes and continues another 40 miles to the start of the kayak leg. Where strong winds and huge swells would once again challenge the racers stamina. Just under 13 hours after the start the first team crossed the finish line and 20 hours later the race ended with the last team crossing the finish. Adventure racing takes good navigators and COC and other orienteering clubs have helped train adventure racers world wide. If you would like more information on adventure racing feel free to contact Justin Yeager at yeagerjustin@hotmail.com

President - Vacant \Vice President/Training Coordinator - Eric Bone, 206-634-2279, e30ne@yahoo.com Treasurer - Katie Sprugel, 206-365-8742, sprugelk@zgi.com Secretary - Ardis Dull, 206-364-4062, ardisdull@juno.com Environmental Coordinator - Rick Hood, 425-640-8134, rick@hoodcs.com \Mapping Coordinator - Debbie Newell, 425-488-3691, dnewel@gte.net \Equipment Coordinator - Don Atkinson, 425-488-0270, dona@atmos.washington.edu Newsletter Editor - Justin Yeager, 253-709-8232, yeagerjustin@hotmail.com Membership Coordinator - Jim Siscel, jsiscel@cmc.net Publicity/Promotion Coordinator - Eileen Breseman, 425-483-6592, breseman@msn.com Land Use Coordinator - Dave Tallent, 425-488-3691, dtallent@gtemail.net Volunteer Coordinator - Vacant \Bill Cusworth, 206-271-0652, bill3@halcyon.com \Jan Urban, 425-402-7225, urbanj@molecumetics.com

Approximately 200 racers from as far away as Hong Kong will be competing against each other and mother nature near Roslyn, Washington this summer. Washington’s very first adventure race entitled The Wuhoo! is scheduled for August 25, 2001.

Adventure racing is a multi-sport event that requires a combination of active outdoor skills such as canoeing, mountain biking, trekking, and orienteering. Labeled as the fastest growing sport in the world. The idea of combining wilderness terrain with an endurance race is capturing the interest of athletes all over North America, as it is an easy cross over for orienteers, mountain bikers, and paddlers.
The Wuhoo! Adventure Race is one in a series of six Wuhoo! races. More information about the series can be found at www.wuhooadventurerace.com
Catch the next Wuhoo adventure race this winter.
Watch for the results of COC members who competed in the Wuhoo in the next issue of Bearing 315!

Meet Results | Cascade’s 2001 Ultimate Orienteer Series
Event Director: Don Atkinson Courses: Eric Bone Registration: Dave Petty Marker Pickup: Eric Bone, Dale Width, Don Atkinson

Eric Bone set some challenging courses on the previously unused western section of the Teanaway Forks area. Courses 3 and 4 had a mass start at 11 am. Course 3 was the same as course 4 for the first 3 controls.
